en.extract:
The squatter pigeon (Geophaps scripta) is a species of bird in the family Columbidae.
It is endemic to northeastern Australia.
According to Australia's then Department of the Environment and Energy, the nominate subspecies, southern squatter pigeon (Geophapa scripta scripta) is listed as vulnerable. It lives in arid and semi-arid areas, including grasslands and dry sclerophyll forests, and nest in shallow depressions in the ground. About 95% of the diet of these birds consists of seeds, as they are known for foraging on the ground.


Distribution
The known distribution of the southern squatter pigeon extends south of the Cape York Peninsula, to the Border Rivers region of northern New South Wales, and the south east of Queensland. The subspecies remains common north of the Carnarvon Ranges, considered a subpopulation. In 2000, the population was estimated at 40,000 breeding birds.


Description
It measures approximately 30 cm in length, weighing between 190 g and 250 g. Adults are mainly a grey-brown, with distinctive black and white facial markings and a white stripe up each of its sides. Blue-grey skin around the eyes, dark brown and patches of iridescent green or violet on the upper wings, blue-grey on the lower breast and belly, and a white lower region. The sexes are similar in appearance.
Juveniles can be distinguished by their duller colouring, less distinctive facial stripes, and paler facial skin.


Subspecies
There are two subspecies of squatter pigeon; the southern (G. s. scripta) and the northern squatter pigeon (G. s. peninsulae). Southerns tend to be slightly larger, and the subspecies can be differentiated by the colour of the skin around the eyes. Evidence suggests that hybridisation can occur where the distributions of the northern and southern squatter pigeons overlap.


References

The '''squatter pigeon''' ('''''Geophaps scripta''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Columbidae]].
It is [[endemic]] to northeastern [[Australia]].<ref>{{cite book |title=The Australian Bird Guide |last1=Menkhorst |first1=Peter |last2=Rogers etc |first2=Danny |year=2017 |publisher=CSIRO |location=Clayton South, Victoria |isbn=978-0-643-09754-4 |page=292}}</ref>
According to Australia's then [[Department of the Environment and Energy]], the nominate subspecies, '''southern squatter pigeon''' (''Geophapa scripta scripta'') is listed as [[Threatened fauna of Australia|vulnerable]]. It lives in arid and semi-arid areas, including grasslands and dry [[sclerophyll]] forests, and nest in shallow depressions in the ground. About 95% of the diet of these birds consists of seeds, as they are known for foraging on the ground.<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution ==
The known distribution of the southern squatter pigeon extends south of the Cape York Peninsula, to the Border Rivers region of northern New South Wales, and the south east of Queensland.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Squatter Pigeon (southern subspecies) - profile |url=https://threatenedspecies.bionet.nsw.gov.au/profile?id=10350 |website=threatenedspecies.bionet.nsw.gov.au}}</ref> The subspecies remains common north of the Carnarvon Ranges, considered a subpopulation. In 2000, the population was estimated at 40,000 breeding birds.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Geophaps scripta scripta |url=https://environment.gov.au/cgi-bin/sprat/public/publicspecies.pl?taxon_id=64440 |website=environment.gov.au}}</ref>

== Description ==
It measures approximately 30 cm in length, weighing between 190 g and 250 g. Adults are mainly a grey-brown, with distinctive black and white facial markings and a white stripe up each of its sides. Blue-grey skin around the eyes, dark brown and patches of iridescent green or violet on the upper wings, blue-grey on the lower breast and belly, and a white lower region. The sexes are similar in appearance.<ref name=":0" />

Juveniles can be distinguished by their duller colouring, less distinctive facial stripes, and paler facial skin.<ref name=":0" />

== Subspecies ==
There are two subspecies of squatter pigeon; the southern (''G. s. scripta'') and the northern squatter pigeon (''G. s. peninsulae'').<ref>{{Cite web |title=Squatter Pigeon |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/squpig1/cur/introduction |website=birdsoftheworld.org}}</ref> Southerns tend to be slightly larger, and the subspecies can be differentiated by the colour of the skin around the eyes. Evidence suggests that hybridisation can occur where the distributions of the northern and southern squatter pigeons overlap.<ref name=":0" /><Gallery heights="190" widths="190">File:Geophaps scripta 0931.jpg|The northern form differs with pink skin around the eye.</Gallery>
